title: Solar thermal hot water: decision guide
subtitle: Flat-plate vs evacuated tube, what fraction of your hot water it covers, and whether MCS certification still matters
excerpt: A solar thermal system heats water using roof-mounted collectors and a twin-coil cylinder. In the UK it covers 50-60% of annual hot water demand. At 2025/26 energy prices, payback runs 15-20 years without a grant. The technology is mature; the case depends on whether you are planning a new cylinder anyway.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust solar water heating guidance. MCS MIS 3001 for solar thermal installation standard. CIBSE TM51 on domestic hot water solar design. EST Solar Water Heating Field Trial 2011 for real-world performance data. BRE Solar Hot Water Systems: Lessons Learnt report. Gov.uk guidance on renewable energy permitted development for solar thermal.

glossaryTerms.definition: A system that uses roof-mounted collectors to absorb solar radiation and transfer the heat to domestic hot water via a heat exchange circuit.
Microgeneration Certification Scheme: the UK certification standard for renewable energy technology installers. MCS certification was historically required for grant eligibility.

content.text: consists of roof-mounted collectors (usually 4 m2 of flat-plate or 20 evacuated tubes), a twin-coil hot water cylinder, a pump controller, and a glycol-filled primary circuit. The collectors heat the glycol, which circulates to the cylinder and transfers heat to the water store. In the UK, a 4 m2 south-facing system delivers around 1,500-2,000 kWh/year: covering 50-60% of a 4-person household's hot water demand of 2,500-3,000 kWh/year. At 27p/kWh electricity avoided, that is a modest cost; at 6p/kWh gas avoided, it is a modest cost.

Flat-plate collectors: a glass-covered aluminium absorber plate in an insulated frame. Lower cost per m2 (around a modest cost installed), lower profile on the roof, more robust. Efficiency drops in cold or overcast conditions. UK industry standard for most domestic installs.

Evacuated tube collectors: glass tubes with vacuum insulation around the absorber. Higher output in diffuse light and cold weather. Individual broken tubes can be replaced. They cost 20-30% more for equivalent output. Worth considering in Scotland, Wales, and upland areas.
South-facing roof with pitch 30-50° preferred; east or west orientation works with a penalty of roughly 20-30% output.
A twin-coil hot water cylinder if you do not already have one. Single-coil cylinders cannot accept a second solar circuit without modification.
A pump and controller. The controller checks collector and cylinder temperatures. It runs the pump only when the collector is hotter than the stored water.
Planning permission is not normally required for solar thermal. It is needed if the property is listed or in a conservation area.
The Renewable Heat Incentive (RHI) ended in 2022. No UK government grant specifically for solar thermal was active in 2025-26.

certification is no longer required for grant eligibility because there is no active grant. It remains a quality assurance standard and some mortgage lenders ask for MCS paperwork. Getting a non-MCS quote is legitimate; compare against the MCS price and verify the installer is Gas Safe or equivalent for any boiler work.

The payback runs 15-20 years at current gas prices. Solar thermal makes most sense when you are replacing a hot water cylinder anyway, when you heat water electrically rather than with gas, or when you are off-grid. Off-grid, solar thermal is more efficient than solar PV via an immersion heater.
